channel.title: Follow Builders RSS
channel.description: 聚合 AI 领域建设者的 X、播客和博客内容。

item.title: If you can make the agents do magic for you and you have taste and agency, then you could be 16, 20, 30, or 45 or 60 and it doesn't matter
item.description:
作者：Garry Tan 账号：@garrytan 简介：President & CEO @ycombinator —Founder @garryslist—Creator of GStack—designer/engineer who helps founders—SF Dem accelerating the boom loop
If you can make the agents do magic for you and you have taste and agency, then you could be 16, 20, 30, or 45 or 60 and it doesn't matter

item.title: sarah achieves the most impossible developer events - she got a bunch of developers at a writers meetup to sit together…. … and actually write 🤯 thanks to @byAnhtho for being our 3rd Writing Mentor! @allenpark helped with AV and will upload both her and @QuinnyPig video soon
item.description:
作者：Swyx 账号：@swyx 简介：achieve ambition with intentionality, intensity, integrity & insanity.
affiliations: - @dxtipshq - @cognition - @temporalio - @aidotengineer - @latentspacepod
sarah achieves the most impossible developer events - she got a bunch of developers at a writers meetup to sit together….
… and actually write 🤯
thanks to @byAnhtho for being our 3rd Writing Mentor!
@allenpark helped with AV and will upload both her and @QuinnyPig video soon

item.title: wow. @Microsoft just appointed a CVP whose entire job is OpenClaw. https://t.co/BpQeniIiVo https://t.co/5BzGvkfvVD

wow. @Microsoft just appointed a CVP whose entire job is OpenClaw. https://t.co/BpQeniIiVo https://t.co/5BzGvkfvVD

item.title: this just became more relevant 🙃 https://t.co/s2Z0P8Mo09
item.description:
作者：Peter Steinberger 账号：@steipete 简介：Polyagentmorous ClawFather. Came back from retirement to mess with AI and help a lobster take over the world @openclaw🦞

item.title: To be clear, I just want PAs who are extremely Claude pilled
item.description:
作者：Dan Shipper 账号：@danshipper 简介：ceo @every | the only subscription you need to stay at the edge of AI
To be clear, I just want PAs who are extremely Claude pilled

item.title: The Stove Guy: Sam D'Amico Shows New AI Cooking Features on America's Most Powerful Stove at Impulse
item.description:
播客：Latent Space 视频 ID：obEWFoLfWD4
We scallop maxed. >> Yeah. >> Thank you, Claude, for telling me the temperature. Useful for everything from deciding where air defense sites are in Iran to scallops, apparently. >> Thank you for having us, Sam. This is a really cool place. Do you want to show us around? >> Yeah, that sounds good. Yes. >> What is this? >> So, this is Impulse Cooktop. Um, it is, to my knowledge, the highest performance stove in the market. Um, >> but, uh, a couple things here that's interesting. So, one is we've, uh, we've set up the station with a ventilation hood as well. um to kind of show you how much power this thing's got available. >> Yeah. >> Notice that the ventilation hood actually turns on automatically. >> Wow. Yeah. >> When we start boiling. But >> look at how fast this is going. So, we're putting 10 we're putting 10,000 watts of power >> into a like mediumiz pan. >> Yeah. >> Or pot. And uh we can boil about a liter of cold water about 40 seconds. This is I think a little more than a liter. Um >> Yeah. >> But you can see it's already starting to boil. already own it. >> Um, and we actually, one thing to note here is we have a partnership with Zephr. Um, so in stores nationwide, starting relatively soon, um, possibly like at the same time this video is posted. Wow. >> Um, there's going to, you're going to be able to buy, >> I'm going to turn this down a little bit. You'll be able to buy, uh, Zephr branded powered by Impulse products alongside the ventilation hoods now. And then we're also previewing features we'll have in the future, which is like coordination between the hood and the stove automatically. So, like I do that, it spins up. >> Yeah. The speed goes to five to two. That's >> And you also Why don't you play with it a little bit just to see how real time it feels? >> And these are magnetic, right? >> They're magnetic. So, you pull it off, it turns it off, actually. >> Wow. >> Oh, wow. Turns off as well. >> So, you have to click it to do it. And then, >> wow, that is so cool. And it's so responsive. It's like very >> We want it to feel analog just like how a gas stove feels because like most other induction stoves can't do this. >> Yeah. It takes a lot. We had to redo the power electronics and control systems from scratch and we control all the the firmware and hardware and everything like that to actually make this experience possible. So everything from the turn of the knob to the bubbles being formed there is under our our full control. >> Wow. Yeah, that's amazing. There's a lot of other things here. Is this testing? I see a lot of pots pan. >> Yeah. Yeah. So when we get interesting context from customers for like my this pan works a little funky or like does it work with X? we end up just going and buying stuff and testing it. We also have a bunch of pots here that are actually wired up with thermouples so we can actually just reliably test like our temperature control power control al and power control algorithms so that like we know that there's no regressions when we go and release stuff to customers. We've actually we're in the process of qualifying an update that like triples the performance of our temperature control system. Wow. >> Which means when I say that it's like what does that exactly mean? It means it gets to the temperature you set about three times faster than it currently does. >> Wow. That's that's very impressive. And I guess for QA, do you guys have like a system where you have a lot of people come in and test out stuff before it goes out or? >> So, we have um we have we we're a small team. We're like 30 people total. >> Um the we QA every build we send out uh to customers obviously. Um and then what we also do is many of us have units at home. And so that's kind of the first wave. So it's like people are cooking at home and they'll see, you know, they'll see the builds, but obviously like for like the baseline, like let's test every edge case, we do it basically here on this station. >> Wow. That's like the true definition of dog fooding. You're like trying it out yourself and making >> Yeah. Yeah. But hopefully you're not eating dog food. But >> that's true. That's true. >> Here we have the >> Yeah. So we basically Yeah. Here's the kind of the stove exploded on a table. But basically we went and like the idea here is like we went and effectively reinvented the stove from first principles around the idea of having a lithium-ion battery at the center of the system. So what does that exactly mean and how does that work? So basically, we put a 3 kWh LFP battery, lithium iron phosphate, inside home appliances, starting with stoves. That gives us an additional 15,000 watts of additional power. So that's like double what a typical induction cooktop has in terms of aggregate power across all the burners and stuff like that >> on top of what the wall supplies through a plug. And so we're able to actually bring to bear significant performance improvements over other appliances just from having more watts watts available. So what we then did is we were like, "Okay, let's reinvent everything upstream of that." So we went and built our own custom power electronics and control system. So like this is one of our induction driver boards. >> Yeah. >> Um we've also got like >> this temperature sensor technology which we'll be using later when we cook. Yeah. >> Um >> we went we and then our induction coil. We actually ended up designing all of this from scratch. Like we did all the magnetic simulation etc. ourselves. And this allows us to actually control everything from the turn of the knob to the pan getting hot. And we control that entire end toend flow with kind of our own firmware embedded systems and and high level software. >> That's so cool. >> Um that has allowed us to basically build the highest performance stove in the market. Um now why do we have all this stuff split out? Well, I also sell this to other major appliance OEMs. So basically we built a modular platform. We call it impulse core. Um, and if you use impulse core, you actually have the you will get the highest performance appliances. You'll be able to build the highest performance appliances yourself as well. So, we've got multiple deals with multiple major appliance manufacturers, some of which have announced, some of which have not announced, and we'll be shipping globally this year u through a number of partners. >> Wow, that's that's exciting. So, you have the main product that you guys build entirely yourself, and then you also have the core product that you sell out. >> Exactly. And that gets us channel access to like every form factor of appliance, every market we may not be into. And also like it gets us into stores across the United States without us having to have like a big sales team. So we can basically operate much more like a software and like a software company than like a hardware company that needs a giant logistics footprint and ships large cardboard boxes across the country and has installation services and all this other stuff. We can really be kind of like intellectually heavyweight but a logistics lightweight basically. Yeah. How long does it usually take for you to build something? Because you even said from the start thinking through first principles kind of reminded of how Elon thought through I think like SpaceX as well. I assume there's a lot of inspiration there. So I guess in terms of building these things out iterating through like what are the timelines looking like? >> Yeah. So it took us four years flat from receiving or no three year it was it was it was it was 2021 we started the company in July 2021. We >> shipped our first renewable units. I believe the first one went to the American Housing Corporation guys in like June 2020 2024 or 2025. So this was uh four three three years and 11 months later was was how long it took end to end to do the entire thing from like company formation >> like literally I filled out Stripe Atlas to ...
